title: Shear-stress-constrained superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates
authors: Liling Sun, Shu Cai, Jinyu Zhao, Qi Wu, Yang Ding, Tao Xiang, Ho-kwang Mao
abstract: Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates exhibit superconductivity under pressure in bulk crystals and under epitaxial constraint in thin films, while remaining highly sensitive to sample quality, oxygen content, defects, and stress conditions. We propose that the metastable RP lattice becomes superconducting only when the local constrained deformation of the Ni-O framework falls within a bounded shear-strain window. This deformation controls octahedral rotations, the interlayer Ni-O-Ni bond angle, and coupling between Ni dz2 and dx2-y2 orbitals. This shear-stress-constrained superconductivity scenario unifies the understanding of the pressure threshold, reversibility, spatial inhomogeneity, pressure-medium dependence, film-substrate sensitivity, and reproducibility.
summary: This paper proposes a new mechanism called Shear-Stress-Constrained Superconductivity to explain why superconductivity in nickelates is so sensitive to external conditions. It suggests that a specific window of shear strain is required to align Ni-O bonds and orbitals correctly. This framework successfully unifies the behavior of high-pressure bulk crystals and strained epitaxial thin films.
main_problem: The difficulty in achieving reproducible superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates due to extreme sensitivity to sample quality, oxygen content, and stress conditions.
main_result: The authors propose the Shear-Stress-Constrained Superconductivity (SSCS) model, which posits that superconductivity emerges only when local Ni-O framework deformation falls within a specific bounded shear-strain window.
method: The paper develops a theoretical framework (SSCS scenario) to unify observations from bulk crystals under high pressure and epitaxial thin films under substrate-induced strain.
model_or_system: The system consists of Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates, such as La3Ni2O7, characterized by a metastable lattice with an intrinsic mismatch between perovskite blocks and rock-salt layers.
key_observables: Transition temperature (Tc), superconducting volume fraction, resistance, diamagnetism (via NV sensing), and local stress tensor components.
important_parameters: Local shear stress, normal stress, oxygen stoichiometry, Ni-O-Ni bond angles, and orbital coupling between Ni dz2 and dx2-y2.
main_assumptions: The RP lattice is treated as thermodynamically metastable and kinetically stabilized.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic comparison of different states: (a) bulk transition via elastic shear strain, (b) a non-superconducting tetragonal phase, and (c) an epitaxial film locked into a shear-strained state by a substrate.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of reproducibility in nickelates, proposes the SSCS model, explains how the model unifies bulk and thin-film observations, and discusses the role of defects and local constraints.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
